questions.question: Inspection particles are manufactured with high permeability but LOW retentivity. The low retentivity matters because it:
options: Makes the particles cheaper to produce
Keeps particles from staying magnetized, clumping together, and clinging to flaw-free surface
Increases the particles' response to weak leakage fields
Allows the particles to survive higher temperatures
questions.explanation: High permeability provides the strong response to leakage fields; low retentivity ensures particles do not magnetize themselves, so they stay mobile and do not build false background on clean surface.

questions.question: A shaft is suspected of having transverse cracks — running around the shaft, across its axis. To make flux cross those cracks at 45–90°, the technician should choose:
options: A head shot, passing current through the shaft
Prods aligned along the shaft axis
A coil shot producing longitudinal magnetization along the shaft axis
A central conductor through a hole in the shaft
questions.explanation: A coil puts flux along the shaft axis, which crosses transverse cracks at roughly 90 degrees; head shots, prods, and central conductors produce circular fields that run parallel to transverse cracks and would miss them.

questions.question: A welder reports the arc wandering erratically while repairing a shaft that passed MT last week. The most likely cause is:
options: Residual magnetism in the shaft deflecting the arc — the part was not adequately demagnetized
Leftover fluorescent particles igniting in the arc
The shaft's coercive force being too low for welding
UV exposure from the inspection booth altering the steel
questions.explanation: Arc blow — the arc deflected by a residual magnetic field — is a classic reason parts are demagnetized after MT; it points to a residual field that was never removed or verified.

questions.question: Which part will be hardest to demagnetize?
options: A low-carbon structural steel plate, continuous method, AC yoke
A hardened high-carbon steel shaft with high retentivity and high coercive force, magnetized with FWDC
An annealed mild-steel bracket magnetized with AC
A part heat treated above the Curie point after inspection
questions.explanation: High retentivity means a strong residual field remains and high coercive force means a strong reversing field is needed to remove it — and deep FWDC magnetization additionally rules out a quick AC-coil fix.

questions.question: After demagnetizing a coil-shot (longitudinally magnetized) part, verification should consist of:
options: Confirming the demag unit's lamp lit during the cycle
Checking with a calibrated field indicator at the ends and corners against the procedure limit — commonly 3 G or less
A single reading at mid-length, since the field is uniform
Waiting an hour and assuming the field decayed
questions.explanation: Residual poles concentrate at ends, corners, and machined features, so that is where the indicator goes; a common acceptance level is about 3 gauss or less, with the written procedure governing, and the readings are recorded.

questions.question: A shop inspects thousands of machined steel pump shafts per week, each needing circular AND longitudinal magnetization with a fluorescent bath. The right equipment class is:
options: A portable AC yoke with aerosol cans
A permanent-magnet yoke and dry powder
A stationary wet horizontal bench unit with head shot and movable coil
Prods fed by a small mobile power pack
questions.explanation: Volume, machined surfaces, fine-flaw sensitivity, and the need for both field directions in one fixturing all point to the wet horizontal bench — head shot for circular, coil for longitudinal, recirculating fluorescent bath.

questions.question: Many aerospace and finished-surface specifications prohibit prods primarily because:
options: Prods cannot generate enough field for thin sections
Prod tips can arc against the part, leaving hard, cracked arc-strike damage
Prods only produce longitudinal fields
Prod amperage cannot be measured with an ammeter
questions.explanation: An arc strike at a prod tip creates a localized hard, brittle (and often cracked) spot — unacceptable damage on finished or fatigue-critical hardware, which is why those specs ban prods regardless of technique.

questions.question: Typical lifting-power performance checks for electromagnetic yokes are:
options: About 10 lb for AC and about 40 lb for DC, at the pole spacing used
About 40 lb for AC and about 10 lb for DC, at any spacing
About 100 lb for both AC and DC
Lifting checks apply only to permanent magnets, not powered yokes
questions.explanation: Typical procedure values require an AC yoke to lift about a 10-pound dead weight and a DC yoke about 40 pounds, demonstrated at the pole spacing being used in the examination.

questions.question: To verify UV-A lamp output before fluorescent inspection, the correct instrument and unit are:
options: A white-light meter reading footcandles at the lamp face
A radiometer reading microwatts per square centimeter at the examination surface
A Hall-effect gaussmeter reading gauss at the filter
An ammeter reading amperes at the lamp ballast
questions.explanation: UV-A intensity is measured with a radiometer in µW/cm², placed at the examination surface or specified check distance — typical procedures require at least 1,000 µW/cm² there.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a function of the conditioning additives in a water-carrier magnetic particle bath?
options: Wetting agents so the bath sheets evenly over the part
Corrosion inhibitors to protect parts and equipment
Antifoam agents to keep the suspension readable
Raising the carrier's flash point for fire safety
questions.explanation: Wetting, corrosion inhibition, and foam control are exactly what water-bath conditioners provide; flash point is a property managed in oil carriers — water does not need one raised.
